problem_summary: 2016 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability/environmental). The task: build a mathematical model measuring a country's ability to provide clean water to its population, forecast India's water security 15 years out (to 2031), design an intervention policy to improve water security, and predict the policy's effect over the same horizon. This O-award team operationalized "water security" as the percentage of population with access to an improved water source and attacked the whole problem with multiple linear regression plus simple time-series forecasting.
models_used: Cross-country multiple linear regression (the core model): access to water regressed on socioeconomic/geophysical predictors over 147-212 countries, data year 2012; built in R

Two-tier model strategy: a 2-predictor 'Basic Model' (population growth + GDP per capita, adj R2 = 0.437) as a baseline, escalated to a 'Refined Model' of 8 predictors selected from a 20-variable candidate pool (adj R2 = 0.716)
Best-subset variable selection via R's regsubsets() (leaps package) to choose the 8-predictor subset maximizing adjusted R2 from 20 candidates
Categorical predictor handling: 'Continent' encoded as dummy/indicator sub-variables (AS/EU/NA/OC/SA) inside the regression
Autoregressive (AR) time-series forecasting in R for population growth, GDP per capita, and sanitation: each predictor's current value regressed on its prior 10 years to project values to 2031

Heterogeneous per-variable forecasting fallbacks where AR data was insufficient: constant-value hold (water supply), constant-percentage-decline extrapolation (electricity access ~3.7%/yr), constant-percentage-growth extrapolation (air pollution ~2.5%/yr)
Out-of-sample validation: re-ran the regression with India removed (leave-one-out style) and compared India's predicted access (83.2% without vs 84.2% with) against the literature value (92.2%)
Climate-change correction layer: linearized a World Bank projection (3 C rise -> 10% water-supply drop in South Asia by 2080s) into 0.044 C/yr and 0.149%/yr access loss, subtracted from the forecast

Intervention sub-model: a second simple linear regression of population growth on women's secondary-school enrollment (G = -0.023589 E + 3.212242; p = 7.38e-8, R2 = 0.2105) to convert an education policy into a population-growth change; sanitation intervention modeled as a +1.41%/yr shift benchmarked against Bangladesh's open-defecation decline
key_techniques: Operationalization of a fuzzy concept: explicitly defined 'water security' = 'access to water' = % population using an improved source, turning an open-ended sustainability prompt into a measurable regression target
Escalating-complexity modeling: deliberately built a deliberately-crude Basic Model first to expose limitations, then justified each refinement, giving judges a clear narrative of model maturation
Data-driven feature selection instead of hand-picking: assembled 20 justified candidate variables then let regsubsets pick 8, which reads as principled rather than arbitrary

Proxy-variable strategy with explicit disclosure: used access-to-electricity as a proxy for 'technological ability' and PM2.5 air pollution as a proxy for water pollution where direct data was unavailable, and named these as proxies up front

Policy levers wired directly into model coefficients: interventions act by shifting predictor values (sanitation +1.41%/yr; population growth -0.24%/yr via women's education), so policy effects fall straight out of the same regression — a clean, defensible intervention mechanism
Benchmarking intervention magnitudes against real-world analogues (Bangladesh sanitation campaign; published condom-use and women's-education statistics) rather than inventing numbers
Scenario matrix / 2x2 factorial reporting: {intervention on/off} x {climate change on/off} = 4 endpoints in one table (94.4 / 98.2 / 92.1 / 95.9), making comparative impact instantly legible

Intellectual honesty as a feature: openly flagged the counter-intuitive positive air-pollution coefficient and the gap between their predicted trend and the 'impending crisis' consensus, then offered a distribution-vs-absolute-supply explanation
why_it_won: "From a judge's seat, this paper wins on disciplined execution and honesty rather than mathematical flash. It (1) nailed the hardest part of a sustainability problem — turning a vague mandate ('ability to provide clean water') into one crisp, data-backed metric, then defending that choice; (2) showed a clean modeling arc (Basic -> Refined -> Forecast -> Climate-corrected -> Intervention) where every step is motivated and the adjusted R2 visibly climbs from 0.437 to 0.716; (3) used real, fully-cited public data (World Bank, Barro-Lee, Harvard CID) across 147-212 countries, which signals genuine effort and reproducibility; (4) actually validated out-of-sample by dropping India and comparing to a literature value, a step most teams skip; (5) connected every policy lever back to a model coefficient so the intervention section isn't hand-waving; and (6) demonstrated rare self-criticism — admitting the model likely measures total available freshwater rather than delivered/distributed water, which reframed a discrepancy into an insight. The writing is polished, the structure is textbook, and the scope (climate correction + multi-pronged interventions + cross-region generalizability) covers the full prompt. Judges reward a believable, well-communicated, end-to-end model over an over-engineered one, and this is exactly that."
section_highlights: "Strong structural moves: (a) A literary hook — the Ancient Mariner 'Water, water, every where / Nor any drop to drink' — opening the summary and immediately reframing India's crisis as a management not scarcity problem. (b) A dedicated DEFINITIONS section operationally fixing every term (access to water, improved source, water security, scarcity) before any math, which preempts ambiguity. (c) An ASSUMPTIONS section split into global + Basic-Model + Refined-Model tiers, including the explicit linear-regression assumptions (linearity, no predictor correlation, normal iid errors) — showing the team knows what its tool requires. (d) Table 1: all 20 candidate variables with units and a written justification each, asterisking the 8 that made the cut — a single table that conveys breadth of thinking and the selection outcome at once. (e) Tables 2 and 3 side-by-side (full model vs India-removed) for the validation, with significance bolded. (f) Table 5's 2x2 scenario matrix and Figures 4-5 line charts isolating 'just sanitation' vs 'just women's education' vs 'both'. (g) A FUTURE WORK section that is specific (replace proxy variables with direct water-pollution data, widen the candidate pool, model inter-predictor causality and droughts) rather than boilerplate. The summary sheet itself front-loads the headline numbers (43.7% -> 71.6% variance explained; 3.78% access increase)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Forecast contradicts domain reality: the model predicts access RISING to 94.4% by 2031, at odds with the well-documented impending Indian water crisis — the team admits the model captures total freshwater availability, not delivery/distribution/treatment capacity, so it answers a subtly different question than the prompt intends
Predicted 2012 access (84.2%) is ~8 points below the published value (92.2%) and the team never fully reconciles this; they lean on it as 'demonstrating trends, not exact values'

Linear-regression assumptions are asserted but not tested: no multicollinearity check (VIF), no residual/normality diagnostics, no heteroscedasticity test — and several predictors (GDP, electricity, sanitation, water access) are plausibly collinear, undermining the no-correlated-predictors assumption

The positive air-pollution coefficient is counter-intuitive (more pollution -> more water access) and is waved away as 'a byproduct of multiple regression' rather than investigated — a likely sign of confounding/collinearity

Proxy variables are load-bearing: air pollution stands in for water pollution and electricity for technology, so the policy conclusions inherit untested proxy validity; the team can't model pollution/technology interventions directly as a result

Weak intervention sub-model: the women's-education -> population-growth regression has R2 = 0.2105 (explains only ~21% of variance) yet a precise 0.24% population-growth reduction is propagated forward as if reliable

Ad hoc, inconsistent forecasting: three variables get autoregression while others get constant-hold or hand-picked fixed growth/decline rates (3.7%, 2.5%), introducing model heterogeneity and analyst-chosen parameters without sensitivity analysis

Cross-sectional coefficients reused for time-series projection: relationships fit across countries in 2012 are assumed stable for India over 15 years — a strong, unverified stationarity assumption the team acknowledges only in passing
Climate correction is extremely coarse: a single World Bank 2080s figure linearized to an annual rate and applied uniformly, with no uncertainty band
reusable_patterns: Operationalize-then-measure: for any vague sustainability/capability prompt, pick ONE defensible, publicly-measurable proxy metric for the target concept and state it explicitly before modeling

Baseline-then-refine two-model ladder: ship a deliberately simple model first to establish a reference R2, then justify each added predictor — gives reviewers a clean improvement narrative and a built-in sanity check

Big candidate pool + automated subset selection: list ~20 justified candidate features in a single table, then use a principled selector (regsubsets/best-subset, or stepwise/LASSO) so feature choice looks data-driven, not arbitrary

Wire interventions to coefficients: design policies as shifts in predictor values so the SAME fitted model yields the policy impact — makes the 'predict the policy's success' requirement fall out for free and stays internally consistent
Benchmark every assumed intervention magnitude against a real-world analogue (e.g. Bangladesh sanitation campaign) and cite it, instead of asserting a number

Leave-one-out / out-of-sample validation: drop the focal entity (here, India) from the fit and compare its prediction to a literature value to argue the model isn't self-fitting — cheap, high-credibility
Scenario matrix table: report a factorial grid of {intervention on/off} x {stressor on/off} so comparative effects are one glance away
Add a stressor-correction layer (climate change) as a transparent, separately-documented adjustment on top of the base forecast, keeping the base model clean and the correction auditable
Disclose proxies and counter-intuitive results openly and explain them — turning potential weaknesses into demonstrations of rigor
Front-load headline quantitative results (variance explained, % improvement) in the summary sheet, and fix all terminology in a Definitions section before any equations
